title: The One Where Old Yeller Dies

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: Hey. Watcha guys doin?
Richard: Monica's making us watch Old Yeller.
---
Rachel: Hey Phoebs, whatcha got there?
Phoebe: Ok, Love Story, Brian's Song, and Terms of Endearment.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 'What are you doing' 또는 'What have you got there'의 구어 축약형
expressions.nuance: 격식 없는 일상 대화에서 자연스럽게 튀어나오는 표현으로, 'what are you'를 거의 하나의 음절처럼 압축해 발음한다. 글로는 watcha / whatcha 두 가지로 표기한다.
expressions.example: 친구가 손에 뭔가를 들고 들어올 때 → "Hey, whatcha got there?"

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: C'mon, you're going out with the guy! There's gotta be something wrong with him!

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yes, 'cause now I have to go down there, and deal with them.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: I remember the day I got my first pay check. There was a cave in in one of the mines, and eight people were killed.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: What're you talkin' about?
Phoebe: C'mon, happy family gets a dog, frontier fun.
Rachel: That's not the end.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: '진짜로?' '말도 안 돼' 또는 '그러지 마' 의 의미로 쓰이는 구어 표현
expressions.nuance: 상황에 따라 설득('C'mon, just try it'), 불신('C'mon, you can't be serious'), 재촉('C'mon, let's go') 등 세 가지로 쓰인다. 이 장면에서는 불신 용법.
expressions.example: 친구가 그 영화 결말을 모른다고 할 때 → "C'mon, everyone knows how that movie ends!"

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: C'mon, you're going out with the guy! There's gotta be something wrong with him!

occ.speaker: Barry
occ.text: C'mon in.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: C'mon!

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: It's Old Yeller, it's a happy movie.
Rachel: What?
Ross: What're you talkin' about?
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '무슨 소리야?' — 상대방 말에 황당함이나 강한 의문을 표현
expressions.nuance: 단순한 정보 요청이 아니라 '그게 말이 돼?' 라는 감정을 실어 쓴다. What are you talking about?의 구어 축약이며, 음절을 빠르게 흘리는 것이 포인트.
expressions.example: 친구가 오늘 시험 없다고 했는데 알고 보니 있을 때 → "What're you talkin' about? The exam is TODAY."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Frankie? What're you talking about?

occ.speaker: Eddie
occ.text: Woah, woah, woah, what're, what're you talkin' about man.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: What're you talking about, everybody loves Joseph.

expressions.dialogue: Monica: Alright, for the bizillionth time, yes I see other women in the shower at the gym, and no I don't look.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: '수도 없이 많은 번째로' — 같은 말을 반복적으로 해야 하는 짜증을 과장해서 표현
expressions.nuance: 'billion'을 가상의 숫자 'bizillion'으로 만들어 과장을 극대화한 유머 표현. 'for the millionth time'도 동일 패턴이다.
expressions.example: 동생이 또 같은 질문을 할 때 → "For the bizillionth time, I already told you where the remote is!"

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Yeah, Ross can't go so it's between my friend Eric Prower who has breath issues and Dan with the poking.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 입 냄새(bad breath)를 완곡하게 표현하는 말
expressions.nuance: 직접적인 'bad breath' 대신 'issues'를 붙여 부드럽게 돌려 말하는 전형적인 미국 완곡어법. 'have __ issues' 패턴은 신체적·성격적 단점을 간접적으로 지칭할 때 폭넓게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: 팀원의 냄새 문제를 상사에게 살짝 알릴 때 → "He's great at his job, but he has some breath issues we should probably address."

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Okay, first of all, breathe. Second of all, I don't get it. Aren't you the one that decided that you didn't want to be with Ross?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: No! (Gets up to leave.) (Under her breath.) If there was I wouldn't tell you.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (Under her breath) God, I woke the beast. Sorry. (To Monica) I was wrong obviously, I just-I misspoke. It's okay.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: C'mon. Keeps his fingers to himself and he's always minty fresh.
Chandler: I don't know, Richard's really nice and everything...
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 입이 항상 상쾌하고 깨끗하다는 뜻; 입 냄새가 없다는 표현
expressions.nuance: 치약·껌 광고 언어에서 온 표현으로, 일상 대화에서는 가볍게 유머러스하게 쓴다. 'breath issues'와 대조되는 긍정 표현이다.
expressions.example: 껌을 항상 가지고 다니는 친구를 칭찬할 때 → "I love sitting next to you — you're always minty fresh."

expressions.expression: older than some people, but younger than some buildings
expressions.dialogue: Chandler: I don't know, Richard's really nice and everything, uh, it's just that we don't know him really well, ya know, and plus he's, ya know, old [Monica gives him a glare] -er than some people, but, uh, younger than some buildings.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 상대방의 눈치를 보며 '늙다'는 말을 엉뚱하게 완화시키는 재치 있는 표현
expressions.nuance: Chandler 특유의 당황해서 즉흥으로 말을 바꾸는 패턴. 망설임(ya know, uh)이 포함된 채 흘러가는 구조 자체가 개그 포인트다.
expressions.example: 교수님 나이를 실수로 꺼냈다가 수습할 때 → "I mean, Professor Kim is... older than some students, but younger than the building we're sitting in."

expressions.dialogue:
Richard: Uh, Phoebe, I don't think your mom would want you to see what's about to happen.
Phoebe: What, what's about to happen?
expressions.speaker: Richard / Phoebe
expressions.meaning: '막 ~하려 하다' — 아주 가까운 미래에 일어날 일을 나타냄
expressions.nuance: will보다 시간적으로 훨씬 임박한 미래를 나타낸다. 드라마나 영화에서 긴장감을 조성할 때 자주 등장하며, 일상 대화에서도 'I'm about to eat'처럼 바로 지금 할 행동을 표현한다.
expressions.example: 회의 시작 직전을 알릴 때 → "The meeting is about to start, you should come in now."

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Or 'You're such a nice guy' means 'I'm gonna be dating leather-wearing alcoholics and complaining about them to you'.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: What about Ben? We can't bring a baby to a hospital.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Rachel you have to read this book. It's called Be Your Own Windkeeper. It's about how women need to become more empowered.

expressions.dialogue:
Richard: Hey, you're gettin' better. I'm gonna keep this by the way.
Richard: [foosball] pass to the middle, lines it up and... BAM! Yes! Could that shot BE any prettier?
expressions.speaker: Richard
expressions.meaning: '이보다 더 ~할 수 없다'는 뜻의 수사 의문문; 극도의 감탄이나 반어를 표현
expressions.nuance: Friends에서 Chandler가 즐겨 쓰는 구조로 널리 알려졌다. 감탄일 때는 긍정 형용사(prettier, more perfect), 비꼴 때는 부정 형용사(more obvious, more annoying)를 쓴다.
expressions.example: 친구가 완벽한 타이밍에 나타났을 때 → "Could your timing BE any better?"

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (to Joey) Could've been a peach.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Excuse me? You don't think I could get a Brian? Because I could get a Brian. Believe you me. ...I'm really not.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: What about Glen? He could be a Glen.

expressions.dialogue: Richard: Nice moustache by the way. When puberty hits that thing's really gonna kick in.
expressions.speaker: Richard
expressions.meaning: '(효과·작용이) 시작되다, 발동하다'
expressions.nuance: 약이나 카페인이 효과를 내기 시작할 때, 냉방기가 작동하기 시작할 때처럼 어떤 것이 본격적으로 기능하기 시작하는 순간을 표현한다. 항상 자동사로 쓴다.
expressions.example: 커피를 마시고 30분 후에 → "The caffeine finally kicked in — I feel so much more awake now."

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Oh come on. It's cold, it's dark, he doesn't know the Village. (Kicks a sign in frustration) And now I have a broken foot. I have no monkey, and a broken foot! Thank you very much.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Julie! Julie, isn't that great? I mean, isn't that just kick-you-in-the-crotch, spit-on-your-neck fantastic?

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: The ball is Janice. The ball is Janice. (goes to kick the ball but kicks Ross's foot instead.)

expressions.dialogue:
Carol: Yeah, a woman I went to college with just became the first female blacksmith down there.
Ross: Well, ya know, they're a little behind the times in Colonial Williamsburg.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '시대에 뒤처지다, 구식이다'
expressions.nuance: 기술·트렌드·사고방식이 현재 흐름을 따라가지 못함을 나타낸다. 사람에게도('He's a bit behind the times'), 장소·조직에게도 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: 팩스만 쓰는 회사를 평가할 때 → "That company is really behind the times — they don't even have email."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Well OK then. [oven timer goes off behind Chandler] Was that the oven timer?

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Figured if we both grew it, we'd look like dorks.
Ross: Yeah, you really sidestepped that land mine.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '(잠재적인 실수나 문제를) 교묘히 피하다'
expressions.nuance: 원래 전장의 지뢰를 비켜 가는 데서 온 비유로, 자칫 상황을 악화시킬 수 있는 결정이나 발언을 가까스로 피했을 때 쓴다. 종종 빈정거림을 담아 쓴다.
expressions.example: 상사 앞에서 실수를 가까스로 막았을 때 → "You really sidestepped a land mine there — good save."

expressions.expression: hog (someone's time / the TV / the bathroom)
expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Hey listen, don't be mad at him, it's our fault. I'm sorry we've been hoggin so much of his time.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: '(시간·자원 등을) 독차지하다, 혼자 너무 많이 쓰다'
expressions.nuance: 돼지(hog)처럼 욕심껏 차지한다는 이미지에서 나온 구어 표현. 의도적이지 않아도 결과적으로 너무 많이 차지했을 때 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: 룸메이트가 욕실을 오래 쓸 때 → "Stop hogging the bathroom — other people need to get ready too."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Ross, let me ask you a question. She got the furniture, the stereo, the good TV- what did you get?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (turns off the TV) Okay..

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Oh my God. Okay, go into the bathroom, use whatever you want, just don't ever tell me what you did in there.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Not to sound too Florence Henderson but, dinner's on the table.
Richard: Ok, just one more point.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: '~처럼 들리려는 건 아닌데, 그렇지만...' — 발언을 부드럽게 예방하는 헤징 표현
expressions.nuance: 말하고자 하는 내용이 상대방에게 잔소리·과잉·권위적으로 들릴까 봐 미리 쿠션을 까는 표현. 'Not to be rude, but...' / 'Not to alarm you, but...' 등 동일 패턴이 많다.
expressions.example: 친구의 발표 준비가 걱정될 때 → "Not to sound like your mom, but have you actually rehearsed that presentation?"

expressions.dialogue: Ross: Ok, we were sitting over there playing on the floor and he grabs the table and he pulls himself up. He pulled himself up. Standing man.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '(무언가를 잡고) 몸을 일으켜 서다'
expressions.nuance: 물리적으로 자신의 몸을 끌어올리는 행동 자체를 표현한다. 아기의 첫 기립처럼 능동적 노력이 담긴 뉘앙스. 비유적으로는 'pull oneself up by the bootstraps(자수성가하다)'도 있다.
expressions.example: 재활 중인 환자가 처음으로 혼자 일어섰을 때 → "After weeks of therapy, he finally pulled himself up without any help."

expressions.dialogue: Ross: I'm so sick of missing stuff. Ya know, I want him for more than, than a day, I want him for a whole weekend.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '~에 완전히 질리다, 더 이상 참을 수 없다'
expressions.nuance: 'be tired of'보다 감정 강도가 훨씬 높다. 오래 쌓인 불만이 폭발하기 직전의 뉘앙스를 담는다. 뒤에 항상 명사 또는 동명사(-ing)가 온다.
expressions.example: 매일 야근이 반복될 때 → "I'm so sick of staying late — I need a vacation."

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Hey, look, you guys, I'm going for anything here, OK? I cannot be a waitress anymore, I mean it. I'm sick of the lousy tips, I'm sick of being called 'Excuse me...'

expressions.dialogue: Phoebe: It just kept getting worse and worse, it should have been called 'It's a sucky life and just when you think it can't suck any more it does.'
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: '계속해서 ~하다' — 반복 또는 지속되는 동작·상태를 나타냄
expressions.nuance: 'kept + -ing'은 단순 과거 반복이고, 'keeps + -ing'은 현재까지 이어지는 반복이다. 'worse and worse'처럼 비교급을 반복하면 점점 심해지는 강조 효과가 있다.
expressions.example: 수리한 차가 또 고장났을 때 → "It just keeps breaking down — I think I need a new car."

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (Angry) I- I- I ca- I can't believe this. I mean, all I asked you to do was keep him in the apartment.

occ.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
occ.text: All right, Carol, I need you to keep pushing. I need-(reaches for an instrument, Rachel's hand is on it) Excuse me, could I have this?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Bye Marcel. See ya on the big screen. You keep people drinkin' that beer, OK. I miss you buddy.

expressions.dialogue: Monica: So what, he's a little older, big deal, I mean he's important to me.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: '별것도 아닌 일' — 누군가가 과잉 반응한다고 생각할 때 쓰는 표현
expressions.nuance: 단독으로 'Big deal.'이라고 하면 강한 빈정거림('그게 뭐 대수야?')이 된다. 'It's no big deal'은 상대방을 안심시키는 겸손한 표현이다. 맥락이 어조를 결정한다.
expressions.example: 친구가 작은 실수를 크게 걱정할 때 → "You're ten minutes late — it's not a big deal, we haven't ordered yet."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: He's right, enough, already. What is the big deal about today? So you slept with her for the first time, so what? You slept with her for seven years after that.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Would you let it go? It's not that big a deal.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Y'know, it wasn't that big a deal. He just balled up socks... and a melon...

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Ok, what the hell happened back there?
Rachel: I don't know, you tell me.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '도대체 무슨 일이야?' — 당혹감이나 불쾌함을 담은 강조 의문
expressions.nuance: 'what happened?'에 'the hell'을 끼워 감정 강도를 높이는 구어 표현. 격식 없는 상황에서만 쓰고, 직장 등 공식 자리에서는 피한다.
expressions.example: 갑자기 앱이 충돌했을 때 → "What the hell? I just saved everything and now it's gone."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: That wasn't a real date?! What the hell do you do on a real date?

occ.speaker: Director
occ.text: And cut. Hey, Butt Guy, what the hell are you doing?

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, then no thanks. What the hell was that? Mental note: If Jill Goodacre offers you gum, you take it. If she offers you mangled animal carcass, you take it.

expressions.expression: tough (= too bad, deal with it)
expressions.dialogue:
Ross: ...if you want to be with me you are gonna have to deal with that.
Rachel: Fine, I will.
Ross: Good, 'cause I love you.
---
Ross: ...tough, 'cause you're gonna have to deal with that.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '어쩔 수 없어, 감수해' — 상대방의 불편함에 대해 동정하지 않겠다는 단호한 표현
expressions.nuance: 단 한 마디 'Tough.'만으로도 '그건 네 문제야'라는 뉘앙스를 전달한다. 자신감 있게, 때로는 약간 차갑게 들릴 수 있는 직설 표현이다.
expressions.example: 팀원이 마감 날짜가 싫다고 불평할 때 → "Tough — the client needs it by Friday and that's not changing."

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Uberveiss. It's new, it's German, it's extra-tough.

occ.speaker: Roger
occ.text: That's tough. Tough stuff. C'mon, Pheebs, we're gonna catch that movie, we gotta get going.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (to Ross): Ahhh, that's alright. Y'know, that's a tough hand to beat.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: So you can't lose, it's there in the title. Wonderfullness is baked right in.
Phoebe: Please, I almost fell for that with Pride of the Yankees.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: '처음부터 내재되어 있다, 기본으로 포함되어 있다'
expressions.nuance: 빵을 구울 때 재료가 안에 들어간다는 이미지에서 온 비유. 소프트웨어·서비스·디자인 등에서 기능이 별도 추가 없이 기본으로 포함된 경우에도 자주 쓴다.
expressions.example: 새 앱에 다크모드가 기본 탑재됐을 때 → "Dark mode is baked right in — you don't have to install anything extra."

expressions.dialogue:
Richard: Uh, the guy was Lou Gehrig. Didn't you kinda see it coming?
Phoebe: ...
expressions.speaker: Richard
expressions.meaning: '(일어날 일을) 미리 예측하다, 낌새를 채다'
expressions.nuance: 'I saw that coming'은 결과가 뻔했다는 뜻이고, 'I didn't see that coming'은 전혀 예상 못 했다는 뜻이다. 부정형이 훨씬 자주 쓰이며 놀람을 표현한다.
expressions.example: 예상했던 실적 발표 결과를 들었을 때 → "Honestly, I saw that coming — they've been struggling all year."

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Right,.. well,.. we never actually got to that... Oh, it was just so nice to see him again, y'know? It was comfortable, it was familiar... it was just nice!

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Alright, I'll tell you what, you call the couch and then, and then we'll call the couch, and we'll see who it comes to.

occ.speaker: Richard
occ.text: See, if anyone overheard that, I didn't come off well.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: I think about stuff. Ya know, I mean, you're at work, you're assembling bones, your mind wanders.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '생각이 딴 데로 흐르다, 공상에 잠기다'
expressions.nuance: 집중하지 않고 생각이 다른 곳으로 떠내려가는 상태를 표현한다. 부정적이기보다는 자연스러운 인간의 상태를 묘사하는 중립적 표현이다.
expressions.example: 긴 회의 중 집중을 잃었을 때 → "Sorry, what did you say? My mind was wandering."

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Well for starters, you may want to light it and lose the spatula.
[Joey holding an unlit cigar and spatula, trying to look like Richard]
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '우선 첫 번째로, 일단' — 여러 이유나 단계 중 첫 번째를 소개할 때
expressions.nuance: 'First of all'보다 가볍고 구어적이다. 뒤에 'and then...' 'also...' 등으로 다음 포인트를 이어가는 흐름에 자연스럽게 쓴다.
expressions.example: 이사할 아파트의 문제점을 나열할 때 → "For starters, the heating doesn't work — and that's just the beginning."

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Well, for starters I would've said the right name at my wedding!

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: ...if you want to be with me you are gonna have to deal with that. Because I know that we're gonna end up together.
Rachel: Fine, I will.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: '결국 ~하게 되다, 마침내 ~의 상태가 되다'
expressions.nuance: 계획 없이 또는 예상치 못하게 어떤 결과에 이르렀을 때 자주 쓴다. Ross처럼 확신을 담아 쓸 수도 있고, 'We ended up eating pizza again'처럼 무계획적인 결과에도 쓴다.
expressions.example: 우연히 같은 팀이 됐을 때 → "We ended up on the same project team — I guess it was meant to be."

occ.speaker: Janice
occ.text: Well then ask yourself this. Why do you think we keep ending up together? New Year's? Who invited who? Valentine's? Who asked who into whose bed?

occ.speaker: Young Ethan
occ.text: Well it's somewhere in between. You see, in a strictly technical sense, of course, I'm not uh..., well I, I mean I haven't ever uh...

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Yeah, so, uh, so, uh, what's the deal with this father guy, I mean, if someone was havin' my baby somewhere, I'd wanna know about it, you know?
